content:
Seaborn was created by **Michael Waskom** as an open-source project first released in **2012**, growing out of the need for attractive, statistically informed plots in the Python scientific stack.

Before Seaborn, practitioners either coded every histogram and regression line in **Matplotlib** manually or used domain-specific tools. Waskom's design bet was simple: **pandas** had become the standard tabular interface, so plotting should accept DataFrames and column names directly.

Seaborn deliberately **does not replace** Matplotlib—it wraps it. Early versions focused on **distribution** and **regression** plots; later releases unified the API around **figure-level** and **axes-level** functions, added **pair plots**, **heatmap** support, and modern **displot**/`histplot`/`kdeplot` replacements for deprecated helpers.

Today Seaborn is the de facto **statistical visualization** layer in ML notebooks: correlation heatmaps before feature selection, pair plots during EDA, and categorical comparisons of model metrics. Matplotlib remains the rendering engine underneath.

timeline.label: 2012 — initial release
timeline.description: Michael Waskom open-sources Seaborn with statistical plotting built on Matplotlib and integrated with pandas.

timeline.label: Mid-2010s — API expansion
timeline.description: Figure-level functions (FacetGrid, factorplot lineage) and regression plots with confidence intervals become standard EDA tools.

timeline.label: 2020s — modern distribution API
timeline.description: histplot, displot, and kdeplot replace distplot; themes and palettes align with contemporary notebook workflows.
